items.q: What is the difference between monetary and fiscal policy?
items.correct: Monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply controlled by the central bank; fiscal policy uses government taxation and spending controlled by the Treasury.
items.misconception: Monetary policy is about money and fiscal policy is about taxes.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same thing — government economic policy.
items.why: While the rough distinction has some truth, the key analytical point is WHO controls each (independent central bank vs elected government) — central to debates on policy independence.

items.q: What is the predicted effect of a binding price ceiling like rent control?
items.correct: Shortage in the market — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price, leading to queues, black markets and reduced quality.
items.misconception: Price ceilings make goods cheaper for everyone with no downsides.
items.random_wrong: Price ceilings only affect the rich.
items.why: Students see price control as obviously beneficial. Standard analysis predicts shortages and unintended consequences — the trade-off is central to evaluation questions on intervention.

items.q: What does the assumption 'ceteris paribus' mean in economic models?
items.correct: 'All other things equal' — when analysing the effect of one variable, all other variables are held constant. It is an analytical simplification, not a claim about reality.
items.misconception: Ceteris paribus means everything happens at the same time in real markets.
items.random_wrong: Ceteris paribus is a Latin name for the equilibrium price.
items.why: Real-world markets never hold all things equal — ceteris paribus is a thought-experiment device. Students treat it as a description of reality, missing why economists use it: to isolate cause and effect.
It's Countries linking worldwide. “Building a wall” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Many countries. “One country” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: iPhone parts come from 4 countries plus assembly in 1 more. Total countries?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
